title: Workflow-Centered Evaluation for Long-Horizon Agent Workflows
abstract: Evaluation practice for language-model agents still tends to privilege the base model as the primary explanatory unit of performance. That assumption becomes increasingly unstable in long-horizon, tool-using, budget-constrained settings, where observed outcomes depend not only on the model, but also on workflow structure, environmental affordances, and resource limits. This paper argues that contemporary agent evaluation therefore suffers from a unit mismatch: practical systems are often discussed as if they were model comparisons, while they are in fact workflow-conditioned system comparisons. We propose a workflow-centered evaluation framework in which performance is treated schematically as a function of model, workflow, environment, and budget. We define five evaluation dimensions—task quality, cost efficiency, recoverability, auditability, and robustness—and operationalize recoverability and auditability as provisional proxy metrics suitable for first-pass empirical study. We situate the proposal within recent benchmark literature on planning, tool use, interactive environments, software-engineering agents, and reflective control. We then introduce a factorized protocol for separating workflow effects from model effects and report a small executable mini pilot on repository-repair tasks. The pilot is intentionally limited: it does not support the paper’s central workflow-dominance hypothesis, but it does show that additional workflow structure can increase trace richness and token cost without improving success on easy tasks. The paper’s contribution is therefore methodological rather than demonstrative. Its aim is to define a more faithful object of measurement and a falsifiable research program for evaluating long-horizon agent workflows.
tldr: For long-horizon, tool-using agent systems, evaluation often measures the wrong unit. The relevant object is frequently the workflow-conditioned system, not the model alone.
hypothesis: On sufficiently long-horizon, tool-using, budget-constrained tasks, workflow variation on a fixed model will often produce larger differences in practical performance than modest model variation under a fixed workflow.
experimentPlan: Use factorized comparisons with fixed-model/varying-workflow and fixed-workflow/varying-model conditions on long-horizon coding, research synthesis, document-grounded retrieval, and resumable administrative tasks. Record task quality, token and latency cost, failure incidence, recoverability proxy metrics, auditability proxy metrics, and perturbation sensitivity at run level. Include explicit perturbations such as tool failures, missing files, noisy retrieval, interrupted execution, and reduced budget ceilings.
conclusion: Benchmark culture remains useful, but it is no longer sufficient for serious evaluation of long-horizon, tool-using, budget-constrained agent workflows. The strongest contribution of this work is a falsifiable reframing of the evaluation problem: workflow-centered claims should be tested directly where statefulness, recoverability, tool use, and budget constraints genuinely matter.
claims: Current agent evaluation often suffers from a unit mismatch between the measured system and the discussed model.
For long-horizon, tool-using, budget-constrained workflows, performance should be analyzed as a function of model, workflow, environment, and budget rather than model alone.
Recoverability and auditability should be treated as first-class evaluation dimensions alongside task quality, cost efficiency, and robustness.
A miniature pilot on simple repository-repair tasks showed that added workflow structure can increase token cost and trace richness without improving success on easy tasks.
limitations: The empirical component is intentionally small and does not adjudicate the central workflow-dominance hypothesis.
The proposed recoverability and auditability metrics are provisional proxy metrics and remain partly evaluator-dependent.
Workflow-centered evaluation may be more expensive and less standardized than leaderboard-style benchmarking.

inspirations.title: PlanBench: An Extensible Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models on Planning and Reasoning about Change
inspirations.note: Motivates careful benchmark design and interpretability of measured capability.

inspirations.title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents
inspirations.note: Frames the need to evaluate LLMs in interactive agent settings.

inspirations.title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?
inspirations.note: Shows the difficulty of long-context, repository-level repair tasks.

inspirations.title: OSWorld: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Open-Ended Tasks in Real Computer Environments
inspirations.note: Highlights realistic computer-environment evaluation for agents.

inspirations.title: Devil's Advocate: Anticipatory Reflection for LLM Agents
inspirations.note: Demonstrates that workflow interventions can materially change outcomes.
